openmeter/notification/eventhandler/noop.purpose: No-op implementation of notification.EventHandler used when the notification subsystem is disabled or unconfigured. All methods satisfy the interface contract without performing any real dispatch, reconciliation, or lifecycle work, enabling Wire to inject a safe zero-value implementation instead of a nil pointer.

patterns.name: Compile-time interface assertion
patterns.description: The file-level `var _ notification.EventHandler = (*Handler)(nil)` ensures the package fails to compile if Handler drifts from the interface, providing the only automated guard against method-set changes in notification.EventHandler.

patterns.name: Blank-identifier parameters, nil return
patterns.description: Every method uses blank identifiers for all parameters and returns nil — no logging, no metrics, no state mutation. This is the canonical noop body shape used throughout the repo (ledgernoop, webhooknoop).
patterns.example: func (h Handler) Dispatch(_ context.Context, _ *notification.Event) error { return nil }

patterns.name: Two-value constructor matching Wire expectation
patterns.description: New() returns (*Handler, error) even though no error can occur, matching the standard Wire provider signature so app/common can swap real vs. noop without changing provider function signatures.

patterns.name: Value receiver on zero-value struct
patterns.description: Handler holds no state; most methods use value receivers to avoid heap allocation and communicate that the struct is safe to copy.
patterns.example: func (h Handler) Reconcile(_ context.Context) error { return nil }

key_file_guides.role: Single-file package providing the noop EventHandler; wired by app/common when notification is disabled.
key_file_guides.watch_for: If notification.EventHandler gains new methods, this file will fail to compile — add matching no-op stubs immediately using blank-identifier params and nil returns.

anti_patterns: Adding logging, metrics, or side-effects to any method — this package must remain intentionally inert.
Removing the compile-time interface assertion (var _ notification.EventHandler = (*Handler)(nil)) — it is the only guard against interface drift.
Using context.Background() or context.TODO() instead of blank-identifying the received context parameter.
Storing any state on Handler — it must remain a zero-value struct with no fields.
Returning non-nil errors from any method — callers in app/common assume noop always succeeds.

decisions.decision: Separate noop sub-package rather than a nil check inside the real handler
decisions.rationale: Matches the broader noop pattern across openmeter (ledgernoop, webhooknoop): Wire injects the concrete noop type so call sites need no nil guards at runtime, keeping business logic free of feature-flag branches.
Handler holds no state so value receivers avoid unnecessary heap allocation and signal that copies are safe — consistent with other zero-value noop structs in the repo.

code_examples.scenario: Adding a new method after notification.EventHandler interface change
